speaker: doctor
text: Good morning. Let's start with your vitals from today's check-in — your diastolic blood pressure is 89 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: Your fasting blood glucose came back at 10.2 mmol/L — that’s elevated, and we’ll want to explore this further.

speaker: patient
text: Oh, really? I’ve been feeling more tired than usual and a bit lightheaded in the mornings.

speaker: doctor
text: Your heart rate is 99 bpm — slightly elevated for resting. Any palpitations or shortness of breath?

speaker: patient
text: Sometimes, especially after walking up stairs. And my hands have felt colder lately.

speaker: doctor
text: Given your symptoms and lab trends, we’ll also consider thyroid function — but first, let’s review your current medications.

speaker: doctor
text: You’re currently on Lisinopril 10 mg once daily for blood pressure control.

speaker: patient
text: Yes, I’ve been taking it every morning with breakfast, no missed doses.

speaker: doctor
text: Have you noticed any dizziness or persistent dry cough since starting it?

speaker: doctor
text: Based on your recent labs and clinical picture, your primary diagnosis is hypothyroidism — we’ll confirm with TSH and free T4 shortly.

speaker: patient
text: That makes sense — I’ve gained about 4 kg in three months without changing my diet.

speaker: doctor
text: Just rechecking your blood pressure — diastolic is now 106 mmHg, which is higher than the 89 we saw earlier this morning.

speaker: doctor
text: And your blood glucose just before lunch was 13.8 mmol/L — quite a jump from the 10.2 we measured earlier.

speaker: patient
text: I did have a sugary coffee this morning — maybe that affected it?

speaker: doctor
text: Possibly, but the rise is still concerning, especially alongside your fatigue and weight gain. We’ll need to assess insulin resistance and thyroid impact on metabolism.

speaker: patient
text: I’ve also been checking my BP at home — last night it was 138 over 89.

speaker: doctor
text: That 89 diastolic aligns with what we recorded first thing this morning — good consistency there.

speaker: patient
text: Right — that reading matches the one you took when I first sat down, the 89 mmHg.

speaker: doctor
text: Given the upward trend in your diastolic pressure and persistent hypertension despite current dosing, I’m going to adjust your regimen.

speaker: doctor
text: I am going to switch you to Lisinopril 20 mg once daily, starting tomorrow. We’ll monitor closely and repeat labs in two weeks.
